title: Tapestry weave: diamond pattern
subtitle: A geometric diamond motif in two-colour tapestry on an upright loom
excerpt: Tapestry weaving builds blocks of colour by weaving individual weft areas separately, not carrying the weft across the full width. This project makes a small panel featuring a two-colour diamond motif on a warp-faced ground, suitable as a small hanging or inserted panel.
sourceNotes: Tapestry weaving technique and the use of interlocking and dovetailing joins are documented in tapestry weaving texts. The diamond motif is a standard geometric repeat used across traditional tapestry traditions. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A weft-faced weave structure in which the weft is beaten down firmly enough to completely cover the warp. Different colours are woven in separate areas to build up a pictorial or geometric pattern.
A full-scale drawing of a tapestry design placed behind or beneath the warp as a guide for weaving the pattern. The weaver follows the cartoon's outlines to place colour areas.

A gap formed in tapestry fabric where two adjacent colour areas turn back on different warp threads without interlocking. Slits are used deliberately in some traditions (Navajo, Kilim) but sewn closed in others.
A join technique in tapestry weaving where two adjacent weft colours link around the same warp thread or around each other at their boundary, closing the slit.

content.text: In
tapestry weave
, the weft completely covers the warp. Different coloured wefts are woven in separate areas, meeting at
interlocked
joins rather than running across the full width. This panel measures 15 x 20 cm finished. The diamond motif is centred on the warp and occupies the middle 10 cm of a 15 cm weaving width.
What you need

items.name: Tapestry wool or rug wool, background colour
Tapestry wool or rug wool, diamond colour (contrasting)
Upright tapestry loom or frame loom, minimum 20 x 30 cm

content.text: Method
Step 1: Warp and prepare the cartoon

Warp the loom at 6 ends per 2.5 cm (enough for a weft-faced weave where weft covers warp completely). Warp the full 15 cm width. Draw a diamond shape on paper at the scale of the finished panel: a diamond 10 cm wide and 12 cm tall, centred horizontally. Place the
cartoon
behind the warp so you can see its outline through the warp threads.
Step 2: Weave the background base

Weave 3 cm of background colour across the full width to establish a firm base. Beat firmly after each pick so the weft covers the warp completely. The warp should not be visible at all from the front: if it is, pack more weft in.
Step 3: Begin the diamond

At the first diamond row, the weft divides into three areas: background left, diamond centre, background right. Wind small butterfly bobbins of each colour. Weave each area separately using the tapestry needle: background left from the left selvedge to the diamond edge warp thread; diamond colour from that thread to the opposite edge; background right from there to the right selvedge.
Step 4: Interlock at the joins
At each boundary between colour areas, use the
interlocking
join: as the background weft returns from the boundary warp thread, loop it around the diamond weft before returning into the background area. This closes the potential
slit
between colour areas. Beat firmly after each set of three picks.
Step 5: Follow the cartoon to complete the diamond

The diamond widens by one warp thread on each side per row for the first half, then narrows by one thread per row for the second half. Follow the cartoon outline: each row the boundary shifts one thread outward (widening) or inward (narrowing). Complete the diamond, weave 3 cm of background above it, cut the panel from the loom, and secure warp ends by knotting in pairs.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems specific to colour-area tapestry.

items.symptom: Visible slits at the colour boundaries
items.cause: Interlocking was missed on some rows.
items.fix: After the panel is complete, sew the slits closed using a blunt needle and a length of either weft colour, stitching through the warp threads at the boundary.

items.symptom: Warp is visible through the weft
items.cause: The weft was not beaten firmly enough, or too few rows were woven per centimetre.
items.fix: Pack the weft in by inserting more picks per row. In weft-faced tapestry, 20-30 weft picks per 2.5 cm is typical for DK-weight tapestry wool. Beat each pick individually rather than in groups.
